organic_treatments: Remove infected plants immediately to prevent virus spread
Spray neem oil (5% solution) every 7-10 days to control whitefly vectors
Install yellow sticky traps to monitor and reduce adult whitefly population
Encourage parasitoid wasps and other natural predators by planting flowering plants nearby
Use reflective mulches (silver-colored) to confuse whitefly insects
chemical_treatments: Spray imidacloprid (0.005%) at 10-day intervals to target whitefly nymphs
Use thiamethoxam (0.01%) for rapid vector control
Apply acephate (0.1%) if whitefly infestation becomes severe
Rotate chemical insecticides to prevent resistance development
Use systemic insecticides like dimethoate (0.03%) for early intervention
preventive_measures: Use disease-resistant or tolerant tomato varieties when available
Control weeds in and around the field as they harbor whitefly
Maintain strict field sanitation and remove infected plant debris
Avoid overhead irrigation that increases humidity for whitefly spread
Isolate infected plants and quarantine for monitoring
notes: TYLCV is transmitted exclusively by whitefly (Bemisia tabaci) - vector control is critical
Virus is not seed-borne but primarily transmitted by infected whitefly
Once established, infected plants may not recover; removal may be necessary
Viral load increases with time - early intervention critical
Chemical treatments target the whitefly vector, not the virus itself

symptoms: Brown circular lesions on lower leaves
organic_treatments: Remove lower infected leaves (>30cm from ground) weekly to reduce spore source
Spray copper fungicide (1% Bordeaux mixture) every 10-14 days
Apply sulfur dust (2-3g per liter) for preventive coverage
Prune and improve air circulation around plants to reduce humidity
Use mulch to prevent soil-borne spore splash on lower leaves
chemical_treatments: Spray mancozeb (0.2%) every 10 days starting from pre-flowering
Use chlorothalonil (0.1%) for early infection control
Apply metalaxyl (0.1%) for severe outbreaks
Alternate fungicides (mancozeb + copper) to prevent resistance
Use azoxystrobin (0.01%) for rapid disease suppression
preventive_measures: Use disease-resistant varieties (EB resistance gene)
Stake and prune plants to improve air circulation
Avoid overhead irrigation; use drip irrigation instead
Remove lower leaves proactively (>30cm from soil)
Practice crop rotation (avoid tomato family plants for 2-3 years)
notes: Early blight is caused by fungus Alternaria solani
Disease is favored by temperatures 24-30°C and high humidity (>85%)
Spores spread through water splash and rain
Infections start on lower leaves and move upward if untreated
Regular scouting (every 3-5 days) is essential for early detection

symptoms: Characteristic white mold on leaf undersides
Firm dark rot on fruit with concentric rings
organic_treatments: Remove and destroy infected leaves and fruits immediately
Spray copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux 1%) every 7 days
Apply sulfur dust weekly for preventive coverage
Improve drainage and air circulation by pruning lower canopy
Avoid overhead irrigation; water at soil level only
chemical_treatments: Spray metalaxyl (0.1%) at first sign of disease
Use mancozeb (0.2%) every 7-10 days for suppression
Apply chlorothalonil (0.1%) in combination with copper
Use cymoxanil (0.075%) for rapid control of new infections
Alternate metalaxyl and mancozeb to manage resistance
preventive_measures: Use resistant varieties (e.g., resistant to Phytophthora infestans)
Improve field sanitation and remove crop residues
Maintain spacing for adequate air circulation
Apply preventive fungicides during high-risk weather (cool, wet)
Practice crop rotation (2-3 year gap from Solanum family crops)
notes: Late blight is caused by Phytophthora infestans (oomycete, not true fungus)
Disease is highly destructive in cool, wet conditions (<20°C, high humidity)
White mold on leaf undersides is diagnostic of late blight
Can spread rapidly in epidemic conditions - immediate action required
Disease affects both foliage and fruit - both must be treated

symptoms: Velvety, raised lesions on fruit surface
organic_treatments: Remove fallen leaves and infected fruit to eliminate fungal source
Spray sulfur dust (2-3g/liter) every 10-14 days starting pre-bloom
Use sulfur-lime spray during dormant season on branches
Prune to improve air circulation and reduce humidity
Apply neem oil during growing season for preventive coverage
chemical_treatments: Spray mancozeb (0.2%) every 10 days from bud break to harvest
Use pentachlorophenol (0.3%) for severe infections
Apply dodine (0.2%) during post-petal fall period
Use captan (0.1%) for early season protection
Alternate fungicides to prevent resistance development
preventive_measures: Use disease-resistant apple varieties (Malus resistant cultivars)
Ensure proper tree spacing for air circulation
Prune and thin branches to reduce humidity inside canopy
Collect and destroy infected leaves during dormancy
Apply dormant-season sulfur sprays in late winter
notes: Apple scab is caused by fungus Venturia inaequalis
Disease requires leaf wetness for 8+ hours during dormancy
Spores primarily from fallen leaves in spring
Preventive sprays more effective than curative treatments
Disease severity peaks in cool, wet spring conditions

symptoms: Bloom on young shoots and fruit
organic_treatments: Spray sulfur dust (2-3g/liter) weekly during growing season
Use neem oil (2-3%) every 7-10 days for suppression
Apply potassium bicarbonate (1.5%) for early infection control
Prune off heavily infected shoots and burn them
Improve canopy air circulation through selective pruning
chemical_treatments: Spray quinol (0.15%) or sulfur-based fungicide
Use triazole fungicides like tebuconazole (0.05%) early season
Apply wettable sulfur (0.2%) every 10 days
Use microbe-based fungicides (Bacillus subtilis) as alternative
Rotate chemical classes to prevent resistance
preventive_measures: Plant resistant or tolerant apple cultivars
Maintain adequate tree spacing for air circulation
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization (promotes soft growth)
Provide supplemental irrigation during dry spells
Remove infected shoots during dormant season
notes: Powdery mildew is caused by Podosphaera leucotricha
Disease thrives in moderate temperatures (15-25°C) with low humidity
Symptoms appear on young leaves first
Disease does NOT require water for infection (unlike other fungal diseases)
Early prevention more important than curative sprays

symptoms: White powdery coating on leaves and shoots
Reduced berry quality and wine flavor
organic_treatments: Spray sulfur dust (3-4g/liter) weekly starting pre-bloom
Use neem oil (2-3%) every 7-10 days as preventive
Apply potassium sulfide solution (0.5%) every 10 days
Prune off heavily infected shoots and destroy
Improve air circulation by strategic canopy management
chemical_treatments: Spray quinol (0.15%) or sulfur-based fungicide every 10 days
Use triazole fungicides (tebuconazole 0.05%) early in season
Apply DMI fungicides (myclobutanil 0.025%) for rapid control
Use wettable sulfur (0.3%) pre-bloom and post-bloom
Alternate fungicide classes to manage resistance
preventive_measures: Use disease-resistant grape varieties when available
Prune to ensure good canopy air circulation
Avoid excess nitrogen that promotes tender shoots
Space rows to maximize sun exposure and air flow
Remove infected fruit and leaves before they spread spores
notes: Powdery mildew is caused by Erysiphe necator (uncinula)
Disease favors 20-27°C with moderate humidity
Infection occurs WITHOUT water on leaves (unlike downy mildew)
Fungicides applied before spore germination are most effective
Regular scouting critical from pre-bloom through véraison

symptoms: Yellow angular lesions on leaf surface
Dense white mold on leaf undersides
Abrupt wilting and blackening of shoots
organic_treatments: Remove and destroy infected leaves and shoots
Spray copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux 1%) every 7-10 days
Use sulfur dust (3-4g/liter) for preventive coverage
Improve drainage to reduce leaf wetness duration
Avoid overhead irrigation; use drip irrigation instead
chemical_treatments: Spray metalaxyl (0.1%) at first sign of infection
Use mancozeb (0.2%) every 7-10 days for suppression
Apply cymoxanil (0.075%) combined with copper
Use fosetyl-aluminum (0.2%) for systemic protection
Rotate fungicides to prevent resistance development
preventive_measures: Use resistant or tolerant grape varieties (V. labrusca, hybrids)
Maintain good canopy air circulation through pruning
Avoid overhead irrigation and early morning leaf wetness
Remove infected leaves weekly during susceptible period
Practice strict field sanitation with plant debris removal
notes: Downy mildew is caused by oomycete Plasmopara viticola
Disease requires leaves wet for 12+ hours for infection
White mold on leaf underside is diagnostic feature
Disease can rapidly defoliate vines if untreated
Preventive fungicides more effective than curative action

symptoms: Circular dark brown lesions on leaves
organic_treatments: Remove and destroy infected fruit, leaves, and canes
Spray copper fungicide (Bordeaux 1%) every 10-14 days
Apply sulfur dust (3-4g/liter) for preventive coverage
Prune to improve air circulation and reduce humidity
Burn infected pruning materials to eliminate fungal source
chemical_treatments: Spray mancozeb (0.2%) every 10 days starting pre-bloom
Use thiophanate methyl (0.1%) for curative control
Apply captan (0.1%) for early-season protection
Use wettable sulfur (0.3%) as protective spray
Rotate fungicides to prevent resistance
preventive_measures: Use black rot-resistant grape varieties (native, hybrid cultivars)
Prune aggressively to improve air circulation
Remove infected fruit immediately to stop spread
Rake and remove fallen fruit and leaves in fall
Apply fungicides preventively when conditions favor disease
notes: Black rot is caused by fungus Guignardia bidwellii
Disease favors warm, wet conditions (24-27°C)
Spores overwinter in infected fruit (mummies) on ground
Infection occurs during wet periods in warm weather
Disease can destroy entire crop if left uncontrolled

symptoms: Tiger-stripe chlorosis and necrosis on leaves
Dark speckling and sunken lesions on berries
Sudden vine decline and reduced vigor
Premature leaf drying in hot conditions
organic_treatments: Prune and remove severely infected arms during dry weather
Disinfect pruning tools between vines to reduce spread
Apply Trichoderma-based wound protectant after pruning
Remove dead wood and improve canopy ventilation
Maintain balanced irrigation to reduce stress on affected vines
chemical_treatments: Apply registered trunk-disease wound sealants immediately after pruning
Use fungicide paint/paste on pruning wounds as per local label
Use foliar protectant fungicide program to reduce secondary infections
Alternate active ingredients to reduce resistance pressure
Follow local viticulture guidelines for Esca management
preventive_measures: Prune in dry conditions and avoid rainy periods
Protect all major pruning wounds
Train workers on sanitation and tool disinfection
Maintain vine vigor with balanced nutrition
Monitor vineyards early for tiger-stripe symptom onset
notes: Esca is a complex trunk disease involving multiple fungal pathogens
Curative options are limited; prevention and sanitation are critical
Heat stress can worsen symptom expression
Early vine management can slow progression
Integrated pruning-wound protection is the most reliable strategy
